title: Intro to Neural Networks
description: From linear algebra basics to a simple multi-layer perceptron

concepts.name: Vectors
concepts.description: Ordered lists of numbers representing points or directions in space. The basic data unit in neural networks.
teaching_hints: Start with 2D/3D intuition before going to higher dimensions
Use concrete examples: RGB colors as 3D vectors, word embeddings

concepts.name: Dot Product
concepts.description: An operation that takes two vectors and returns a single number measuring their similarity. The core computation inside every neuron.
teaching_hints: Connect to similarity: parallel vectors have large dot products
Show the geometric interpretation (projection) before the formula

concepts.name: Matrices
concepts.description: 2D grids of numbers that represent transformations. Neural network layers are matrix operations.
teaching_hints: Frame as 'a bunch of dot products happening at once'
Use small 2x2 examples before generalizing

concepts.name: Matrix Multiplication
concepts.description: Combining two matrices by taking dot products of rows and columns. This is how data flows through a neural network layer.
teaching_hints: Walk through a 2x2 example step by step
Emphasize: each output element is a dot product of a row and column

concepts.name: Weighted Sum
concepts.lesson_title: How a Neuron Weighs Its Inputs
concepts.description: Multiplying each input by a weight and adding them up. This is what a single neuron does to its inputs before applying an activation.
teaching_hints: Frame as 'the neuron is voting on how important each input is'
Connect back to dot product: weighted sum IS a dot product of inputs and weights

concepts.name: Activation Functions
concepts.description: Non-linear functions (ReLU, sigmoid, tanh) applied after the weighted sum. Without these, stacking layers would be useless because linear(linear(x)) is still linear.
teaching_hints: Start with the why: 'why can't we just stack linear layers?'
ReLU is the easiest to understand: max(0, x). Just clips negatives.

concepts.name: Single Neuron
concepts.description: The fundamental unit: takes inputs, computes a weighted sum, adds a bias, applies an activation function, produces one output.
teaching_hints: Draw the diagram: inputs -> weights -> sum -> activation -> output
Compare to biological neurons (loosely) for intuition

concepts.name: Forward Pass
concepts.description: Feeding input data through the network layer by layer to produce an output. Data flows forward, each layer transforms it.
teaching_hints: Trace a concrete input through a tiny 2-layer network by hand
Each layer is: output = activation(weights @ input + bias)

concepts.name: Loss Functions
concepts.description: Functions that measure how wrong the network's output is compared to the correct answer. MSE for regression, cross-entropy for classification.
teaching_hints: Start with intuition: 'we need a single number that says how bad our prediction was'
MSE: square the errors so big mistakes count way more than small ones

concepts.name: Derivatives
concepts.description: The rate of change of a function. Tells you which direction to adjust a parameter to reduce the loss.
teaching_hints: Use the slope/hill-walking analogy: the derivative tells you which way is downhill
You don't need to be a calculus expert, just understand: derivative = direction of steepest change

concepts.name: Chain Rule
concepts.description: How to compute derivatives through composed functions. The mathematical foundation of backpropagation.
teaching_hints: Use the 'chain of gears' analogy: if gear A turns gear B which turns gear C, how fast does C turn when A turns?
f(g(x))' = f'(g(x)) * g'(x). Each link in the chain multiplies.

concepts.name: Backpropagation
concepts.description: The algorithm that computes how much each weight contributed to the error, by applying the chain rule backwards through the network.
teaching_hints: Start with a 2-layer example and trace the gradient backwards
Key insight: we reuse intermediate results (dynamic programming) to avoid redundant computation

concepts.name: Gradient Descent
concepts.lesson_title: Walking Downhill to Find the Answer
concepts.description: The optimization algorithm: compute gradients via backprop, then nudge each weight a small step in the direction that reduces the loss.
teaching_hints: The 'walking downhill in fog' analogy: you can't see the bottom, but you can feel the slope under your feet
w_new = w_old - learning_rate * gradient. That's the whole update rule.

concepts.name: Learning Rate
concepts.lesson_title: How Big a Step to Take
concepts.description: How big a step to take during gradient descent. Too large: overshoot and diverge. Too small: take forever to converge.
teaching_hints: Use the hiking analogy: giant steps might jump over the valley, tiny steps take all day
In practice, people use learning rate schedulers that start big and shrink over time

concepts.name: Multi-Layer Perceptron
concepts.description: A neural network with multiple layers: input -> hidden layers -> output. The simplest deep learning architecture. Putting it all together.
teaching_hints: This is the payoff: everything you learned connects here
Walk through a complete cycle: forward pass -> loss -> backprop -> update weights -> repeat
